content.method: Randomized or quasi-experimental variation in admissions criteria transparency/cost across pilot districts, with pre/post tracking of applicant SES composition; qualitative interviews with low-SES families on decision-making to isolate self-exclusion versus access-barrier mechanisms.
content.question: Does admissions-process reform (e.g., reducing reliance on private exam preparation) meaningfully reduce SES-linked selection into 6/8-year tracks, or does it merely shift selection to another SES-correlated proxy while leaving parental self-exclusion behavior unaddressed?
content.why_it_matters: T3 is politically the most feasible option per multiple lenses, but its effectiveness hinges entirely on whether procedural reform can reach the behavioral (self-exclusion) rather than purely mechanical (exam-prep access) drivers of unequal participation. If self-exclusion dominates, T3 will show weak real-world equity effects despite lower political cost.
